#  \*= Assignment {#assignment}

### Purpose

Multiplies a numeric variable with a numeric expression.

### Syntax

x **\*=** y

*x:numeric variable\
y:aexp*

### Description

x \*= y multiplies the value in variable x with the expression y.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

OpenW # 1 
Dim x# = 6 
x *= 9 
Print x  // Prints 54 
```

### Remarks

Although \*= can be used with any numeric variable, the usage of integer
variables is recommended in order to achieve the maximum optimization
for speed.

The following can be used instead of x\*=y:

x = x \* y

x := x \* y

Mul x , y

When integer variables are used \*= doesn\'t test for overflow!

### See Also

[+](add_op.htm), [-](sub_op.htm), [\^](pow_op.htm), [\*](mul_op.htm),
[/](2dmlrd.htm), [\\](2dmlrd.htm#div_op), [%](mod_op.htm),
[Add](add.htm), [Sub](sub_f.htm), [Mul](mul.htm), [Div](div.htm),
[++](hddnjz.htm), [\--](4zbkbi.htm), [+=](txfdrs.htm), [-=](8u3xoa.htm),
[/=](aksmwd.htm) , [\*=](#.22bki), [Operator Hierarchy](operators.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 24/06/2017 by James Gaite}

#  ListBox Control {#listbox-control}

### Purpose

Creates a ListBox control in the current active form, window, or dialog.

### Syntax

**ListBox** text\$, id%, x, y, w, h\[, style%\]

*text\$:control text\
id%:control identifier\
x, y, w, h:iexp\
style%:the control styles*

### Description

The control is a rectangle containing a list of strings (such as
filenames) from which the user can select.

| *Style* | *Meaning* |
|:---|:---|
| LBS_NOTIFY (\$0001) | sends a message to the parent window when the user selects an entry by clicking. |
| LBS_SORT (\$0002) | performs an alphabetical sort of several alternatives. |
| LBS_NOREDRAW (\$0004) | prevents ListBox redraw after receiving changes. |
| LBS_MULTIPLESEL(\$0008) | after an initial selection (the entry is displayed in reverse) enables additional selections. The number of selections is not limited. |
| LBS_OWNERDRAWFIXED(\$0010) | specifies a ListBox, whose input must be performed by the calling task. All items within this ListBox have the same height. |
| LBS_OWNERDRAWVARIABLE (\$0020) | similar to LBS_OWNERDRAWFIXED, except that the items can have a variable height. |
| LBS_HASSTRINGS (\$0040) | used when items within a ListBox are composed of strings. The ListBox refers items to strings by using pointers. |
| LBS_USETABSTOPS (\$0080) | displays a multi-column ListBox, whereby the individual columns are located at predefined tab positions. |
| LBS_NOINTEGRALHEIGHT(\$0100) | makes the size of the ListBox the size specified by application. |
| LBS_MULTICOLUMN(\$0200) | specifies a multi-line ListBox, which can scroll horizontally. |
| LBS_WANTKEYBOARDINPUT(\$0400) | allows for assignment of special keys or key combinations (Hotkeys) to entries in a Listbox. |
| LBS_EXTENDEDSEL(\$0800) | specifies a ListBox, whereby multiple entries can be selected by using the Shift key and mouse clicks. |
| LBS_STANDARD | creates a ListBox with the following attributes: LBS_NOTIFY \| LBS_SORT \| WS_VSCROLL \| WS_BORDER |

If you do not specify a style, the default style is LBS_NOTIFY \|
WS_BORDER.

The command creates a control without an OCX wrapper; so it and cannot
be handled using properties, methods, and event subs. When used in a
form the WM_COMMAND message should be handled in the form\'s
\_**Message** sub.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Dlg 3D On 
Local x% , sel$ 
Dialog # 1 , 10 , 10 , 200 , 310 , "Test" 
ListBox "Listbox" , 10 , 20 , 20 , 150 , 200 
DefPushButton "OK" , IDOK, 10 , 250 , 80 , 25 , WS_TABSTOP 
PushButton "CANCEL" , IDCANCEL, 110 , 250 , 64 , 24 , WS_TABSTOP 
EndDialog 
Data "GFA-BASIC 32" 
Data "GFA-BASIC for MS-DOS" 
Data "GFA-BASIC for Windows" 
Data "GFA-BASIC for Atari" 
Data "GFA-BASIC for Amiga" 
For x% = 0 To 4 
Read sel$ 
~ SendMessage(Dlg( 1 , 10 ), LB_ADDSTRING, 0 , sel$ ) 
_Win$ (DlgItem( 1 , 10 )) = sel$ 
Next 
ShowDialog # 1 
Do 
Sleep 
Until Me Is Nothing 
Dlg 3D Off 
 
Sub Dlg_1_Message ( hWnd% , Mess% , wParam% , lParam% ) 
Switch Mess 
Case WM_COMMAND 
Switch LoWord( wParam ) 
Case 10 
Trace "Notification code: " & HiWord( wParam ) 
Case IDOK, IDCANCEL 
CloseDialog # 1 
EndSwitch 
EndSwitch 
EndSub 
```

### Remarks

This command is particular useful for a dialog box in a GLL, because a
GLL doesn\'t support OCX controls.

With the general **Control** statement any control type can be created.

### See Also

[Control](_4.htm), [AnimateCtrl](animatectrl.htm),
[AutoCheckBox](autocheckbox.htm),
[AutoRadioButton](autoradiobutton.htm), [CheckBox](84bk9r.htm),
[ComboBox](37os9y.htm), [CText](ctext.htm), [Dialog](dialog.htm),
[DefPushButton](defpushbutton.htm), [EditText](edittext.htm),
[GroupBox](groupbox.htm), [HeaderCtrl](headerctrl.htm),
[ListBox](#.2vfxf), [ListViewCtrl](listviewctrl.htm),
[LText](ltext.htm), [ProgressCtrl](progressctrl.htm),
[PushButton](pushbutton.htm), [RadioButton](radiobutton.htm),
[RichEditCtrl](richeditctrl.htm), [RText](rtext.htm),
[ScrollBar](scrollbar.htm), [StatusCtrl](statusctrl.htm),
[TabCtrl](tabctrl.htm), [ToolBarCtrl](toolbarctrl.htm),
[TrackBarCtrl](trackbarctrl.htm), [TreeViewCtrl](treeviewctrl.htm),
[UpDownCtrl](updownctrl.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/10/2014 by James Gaite}

#  \|\| Logical operator {#logical-operator}

### Purpose

Logical OR of a true/false status of two values

### Syntax

i **\|\|** j

*i, j:function arguments*

### Description

To test whether either of two conditions is true (or if both are true),
use GFA-BASIC 32\'s logical OR operator **\|\|**. The condition is
evaluated form left to right. To evaluate to true only one of the
conditions must be true. When the first condition is true, the second
isn\'t evaluated.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Dim a% = 10 
If a% = 0 || ff () Then Print "Both evaluated" 
If a% || ff () Then Print "Only one evaluated" 
 
Function ff () As Int 
Return 1 
EndFunction 
|| is a logical OR operator (and can be replaced by OR) but is not the same as the bitwise OR operators |, or %|. Replacing || with either of these would first evaluate both conditions, which are then bitwise Or-ed. Then the result of the bitwise or operation is tested for true or false, as shown below:
Dim a% = 10 , b% = 2 
Print a% = 10 Or b% = 5 // Prints -1 (True) 
Print a% = 10 || b% = 5 // Prints True 
Print a% = 10 %| b% = 5 // Prints False 
Print a% = 10 | b% = 5  // Prints False 
```

### See Also

[If](if.htm), [&&](amp2.htm), [\^\^](82dcaw.htm), [!](negation_op.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 23/09/2014 by James Gaite}

#  The Imports tab {#the-imports-tab}

The \'Imports\' windows displays all elements that are imported using
the [\$Library](library.htm) command for compiled GFA-BASIC 32 library
files (.lg32).

![](../images/ImportsTab.png){border="0"}

### General Description

As can be seen from the picture above, imported elements are displayed
in collapsible or foldable groups according to their type which are:

- Procs (including Functions);
- Variables (including Hash Arrays);
- Enums (and Constants);
- Declares (APIs); and
- Types

Initially the \'Procs\' group, showing all the imported Procedures and
Functions, is the only \'unfolded\' group.

### More Information

More information can be gleaned from the Imports list by both hovering
over and clicking the relevant entry.

Clicking on the entry displays the name of the entry along with any
optinal description in the IDE\'s status; in addition, when an \'Enum\'
entry is clicked, the value of the entry is also displayed.

Hovering the mouse over an entry gives even more information in a small
pop-up box next to the item (see the picture above). The information
differs according to the item type as follows:

- **Procs** - The Procedure, Function or Sub declaration, the
  description (if added in the Library) and finally the name of the
  parent library.
- **Variables and Enums** - The Varaible declaration including Type and
  initial value, the description (if added in the Library) and then the
  name of the parent library.
- **Declares** - The API declaration, the description (if there is one)
  and the parent library./LI\>
- **Types** - The full Type declaration showing element names and types,
  the optional description (if any) and, lastly, the parent library.

### Known Issues

There are a few problems when displaying details of an item when the
mouse hovers over it:

1.  *\[Fixed in version 2.54\]* With all import types (except Types), if
    there is no optional description, the library name is also omitted.
2.  *\[Fixed in version 2.54\]* For a variable or a constant, the
    library name is given twice, once after the variable declaration
    line AND then again at the end.
3.  *\[Fixed in version 2.54\]* For a Declare, the optional description
    appears twice after the declaration line.\[Reported by James Gaite,
    20/02/2019\]

### See Also

[The Files tab](4cex04p.htm), [The Procs tab](fq66o6.htm), [The
Properties Tab](qe6miu.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 26/02/2019 by James Gaite}

#  Mci\$ Function {#mci-function}

### Purpose

Executes a Mci (Multimedia Control Interface) command.

### Syntax

**err = Mci\[\$\]**(cmd\$ \[, formvar\])

*cmd\$sexp\
wininteger expression*

### Description

**Mci\$**(*cmd\$*) executes a Mci command (as **Mci\$**(\"status id
mode\")). An error (in the command string, or any other error) is not
reported with a message box, but returned as result *err*. (-1 if the
mmsystem could not be found).

With **mciErr\$**(*err*) you get the descriptive error text which would
have been displayed for the **Mci** command.

**Mci\$**(*cmd*\$, *formvar*) Does the same as **Mci\$**(sexp). The
window (form object) given in integer expression (Win_1) gets a
MM_MCINOTIFY message (\$3b9) when the mci command finished execution
(dummy\$=**Mci\$**(\"play id notify\",Win_1)).

The MM_MCINOTIFY message can be handled in Win_1\_**MciNotify**(devID%,
Code%) event sub.

### Remarks

The MM_MCINOTIFY message (\$3b9). The Code% is returned in wParam.

wParam=1 - Mci command aborted

wParam=2 - Mci command successful

wParam=4 - Mci superseded by a new notify command

wParam=8 - Mci error, not reported when using **Mci\$**()

**LoWord**(lParam) = Device ID (devID%) sending the message.

(the notify message is not sent, if the Mci returned an error in \_EAX.)

### See Also

[Mci](mci.htm), [mciErr](mcierr.htm)\$, [mciID](mciid.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/05/14 by James Gaite}

#  Variables, Data Types and Constants {#variables-data-types-and-constants}

In GFA-BASIC 32, you use *variables* to temporarily store values during
the execution of an application. Variables have a name (the word you use
to refer to the value the variable contains) and a data type (which
determines the kind of data the variable can store).

*Constants* also store values, but as the name implies, those values
remain constant throughout the execution of an application. Using
constants can make your code more readable by providing meaningful names
instead of numbers. There are a number of built-in constants in
GFA-BASIC 32, but you can also create your own.

*Data types* control the internal storage of data in GB32. By default,
GB32 uses the **Variant** data type. There are a number of other
available data types that allow you to optimize your code for speed and
size when you don\'t need the flexibility that **Variant** provides.

[Variables](variables.htm)

[Data Types](xiaaa2.htm)

[Literals](literals.htm)

[Variant Data Type](variant.htm)

[Operator Hierarchy](operators.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 23/08/2020 by James Gaite}

#  Keyboard Accelerators {#keyboard-accelerators}

**Moving the cursor**

The editing in the GFA-BASIC editor is supported by a whole range of
keyboard commands. Using the cursor block results in the following:

|  |  |
|:---|:---|
| Arrow left | move cursor one character left |
| Arrow right | move cursor one character right |
| Arrow up | move cursor one line up |
| Arrow down | move cursor one line down |
| End | move cursor to line end |
| Home | move cursor to the first character of the line. Press twice to place the cursor at the beginning of the line. |
| Pg up | scroll one page up |
| Ctrl + Pg up (+ Shift) | Jumps (and selects) to the first line of the current Proc, or if already there to the previous Procedure/Sub/Function. |
| Pg down | scroll one page down |
| Ctrl + Pg down (+ Shift) | Jumps (and selects) to first line of the next Procedure/Sub/Function. |
| Ctrl + End | move cursor to end of file |
| Ctrl + Home | move cursor to start of file |

Holding down Shift together with some of the keyboard shortcuts above
will result in selecting a range of characters, words, and lines.

The cursor can also be moved by using the mouse. To do this, move the
mouse pointer to the desired position and click the left mouse button
once.

**Mouse Button Commands**

|  |  |
|:---|:---|
| Left-DblClick Ctrl + Left-DblClick Shift + Left-DblClick Shift + Left-Click (in margin) | Locates the next word under the mouse pointer, no change here. Improved: locates all occurences of a variable, whether or not a postfix is used. Locates the previous word under the mouse. Reverse of Left-DblClick. Selects a word. Same as Ctrl+W. Toggles one of seven breakpoints. |

**Unnamed bookmarks**

|  |  |
|:---|:---|
| Shift + Ctrl + Up | Set unnamed bookmark |
| Shift + Ctrl + Down | Set unnamed bookmark |
| Ctrl + Arrow Up | move cursor up to line with bookmark |
| Ctrl + Arrow Down | move cursor down to line with next bookmark |
| Mouse | click with left mouse button in the margin in the editor to set or remove a boomark. |

**Control Keys**

A range of editor functions can be invoked by pressing together the
control key (Ctrl,\^) and a character, without having to open the
corresponding menu first. Furthermore, you can invoke editor functions
which are not implemented in the menus as follows:

<table style="width:97%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 48%" />
<col style="width: 48%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + A</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Selects all text</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + B</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Toggle Bookmark</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + C</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Copy selection to clipboard.<br />
(Ctrl + Delete)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + D</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">[Since IDE v2.6.2.2626] Duplicate the
current line.<br />
[Before IDE v2.6.2.2626] Toggle Debug output window (same as
Alt-3).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + F</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Search text. Use F3 to find the next
occurrence of the text. Use Shift + F3 to find the previous
occurrence.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + G</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">invokes a Dialog box for entry or a line
number. The cursor then jumps to this line.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + Ctrl + H</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Displays a Prompt to provide a memory
address from which to display a HexDump. The address can be followed by
a comma and a value for the number of bytes to show (default = 256
bytes).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + I</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Insert ° comments, current line only
without a selection.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">App + I</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shows a context menu to insert
miscellaneous: Gfa_FileName, ..., and Date (see also Shift + Ctrl +
O).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + J</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Remove ° comments, current line only
without a selection.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + K</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Invokes the set bookmarks context
menu.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + M</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Starts Extension Manager.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + N</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">inserts a blank line above the line with
the cursor.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + Ctrl + N</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Inserts a new procedure (Proc, Sub,
Function, FunctionVar) without unfolding surrounding procedures.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + O</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">inserts the last portion of a line
previously deleted with Ctrl+P at the current cursor position.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + Ctrl + O</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shows a context menu to insert
miscellaneous: Gfa_FileName, ..., and Date (since IDE v.2.6.2.2626 -
same as App + I)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + P</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">deletes the remainder of the current line
from cursor position.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">App + P</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Inserts the actual procedurename into the
text.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + Q</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Invokes the bookmarks selection context
menu.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + R</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Replace text. Use Ctrl+F3 to replace the
next occurrence of the text. Use Ctrl + Shift + F3 to replace the
previous occurrence.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + S</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Save current file.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + T</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Transpose characters (swaps the character
at the left with the character at the right).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + U</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">performs an "undelete line". The last
deleted line (Ctrl + Y) is inserted back into the text at the current
position.<br />
After a Ctrl+U the recovered line remains in the internal buffer. This
means that after a Ctrl+Y the function Ctrl+U can be invoked repeatedly
to perform a primitive copy of the deleted line.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + V</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Paste clipboard contents (Shift +
Insert)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + W</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Selects current word (same as
Shift+Left-DblClick).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + X</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Cut selection to clipboard. (Shift +
Delete)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + Y</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">deletes the line with the cursor.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + Z</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Undo (Ctrl + Backspace).</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

**Function keys**

<table style="width:97%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 48%" />
<col style="width: 48%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F1</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Keyword Help</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F1</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Index Help</td>

<td style="text-align: left;">F3</td>

<td style="text-align: left;">F4</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Next line containing a syntax error</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F4</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Previous line containing a syntax
error</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F5</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Compile and Run</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F5</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Compile only (Test). This performs a full
compile to test whether all loops, subroutines, and conditional
statements of the program are complete. It also collects all variables
and checks their correct use.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + F5</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Saves the current file (when Dirty) and
then compiles (Test) the program.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Alt + F5</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Saves the current file and then compiles
the program to a stand-alone EXE, GLL, or Lg32. If the program file has
been changed - due to changing compile settings - it is saved after
compiling.<br />
This is a shortcut to Ctrl-S (save) &gt; Ctrl-E (compile) &gt; Ctrl-S
(save), including checks for Gfa_Dirty and success.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F6</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Cycle through the sidebar tabs from left
to right. Activates the sidebar when it isn't visible.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F6</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Cycle through the sidebar tabs from right
to left. Activates the sidebar when it isn't visible.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + F6</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">1. Cycle through the sidebar and code
editor. Activates the sidebar when it isn't visible.<br />
2. Cycle through forms in the Form Editor.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + Ctrl + F6</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">In the Form Editor cycles backwards
through the forms.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F7</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Toggle between the code and form editor.
Activates the sidebar when it isn't visible.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F7</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Creates a new form in the form editor.
Activates the sidebar when it isn't visible.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + F7</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Toggles format lines delineating tabbed
code structures.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F10</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Activate the menu bar</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F11</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Toggle folding of current procedure.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F11</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Selects the current Proc (or Sub,
Func).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + F11</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Sets the Proc to the top of the
editor.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Alt + F11</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Prints the current Proc. (Selects the proc
and invokes Gfa_Print).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">F12</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Toggle folding of current procedure and
all below.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Shift + F12</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Folds all procedures, sets the caret to
the first line of the current Proc.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + F12</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Folds all procedures, except the current
Proc.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Alt + F12</td>
<td style="text-align: left;">Disassembly of the current Proc in the
output window.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

**Alt keys**

These key combinations are a shortcut for some of the menu items.

|  |  |
|:---|:---|
| Alt + 0 | Switch to Code Editor ( F7) |
| Alt + 1 | Switch to Form Editor ( F7) |
| Alt + 2 | Enable OCX Properties sidebar |
| Alt + 3 | Toggle Debug Output Window |
| Alt + 4 | Split window |
| Alt + 5 | OCX Overview for tab order (TreeView overview of all Forms) |
| Alt + Backspace | Undo ( Ctrl +Z) |
| Alt + Return | Properties dialog box. |
| Alt + Ctrl + R | Record keys, or end recording when recording has started. |
| Alt + Ctrl + P | Play recorded keyboard macro, or pause recording when recording has started. |

**Other keys**

<table style="width:97%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 48%" />
<col style="width: 48%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Insert</td>
<td>Toggle between insert and overwrite mode</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Delete</td>
<td>delete the character under cursor and move the remainder of the line
left.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Esc</td>
<td>cancels the Auto Complete Listbox, or cancels the selection with an
active selection or, without an active selection, brings you back to the
previous position in the history list (same as Ctrl+ -).<br />
<strong>OR</strong> stops searching in Incremental Search mode.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Tab</td>
<td>Indent the selected text one tab stop (8 spaces) to the right. In
overwrite mode Tab moves the cursor in multiples of 8 to the end of the
line.<br />
TabWhen sidebar has the focus,resets the focus to the editor
window.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + Tab</td>
<td>Jump in multiples of 8 to the beginning of the line.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + Break</td>
<td>Break program (Stop)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Ctrl + Shift + Break</td>
<td>Break and continue using the debugger</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: left;">Enter</td>
<td>When in the sidebar jumps to the first line of the selected
procedure, or inserts the text of the Import element.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 17/02/2023 by James Gaite}

#  Create Exe/Compile To Exe {#create-execompile-to-exe}

After selecting Compile To Exe the following dialog box is displayed.
Depending on the type of the source code (normal program, a GLL, or a
library) two or three tabs are showed.

After filling in the dialog box click OK to start compiling. In
addition, the information provided in the dialog box is saved in the
project file in memory. Now the project is extended with the compile
information, so it must be saved again to make the compile information
persistent.

![](../images/CompileToExe.jpg){border="0"}
![](../images/CreateExe-VersionPage.jpg){border="0"}

### Program tab

When compiling a normal program to a stand-alone executable this dialog
box is displayed. This is a system property dialog box and shows the
language of your Windows installation.

**Source** -- Shows the name of the file currently loaded.

**Change Exe** -- Shows the name of the stand-alone executable. To
initialize the EXE name to the name of the source code file name, click
the \'Init Progname\' button. When you click the button displaying the
EXE\'s name a file selection dialog box pops up which you can use the
provide a different name.

**Change Icon** -- Each EXE must contain an icon used to show with the
file name in the Explorer.

**Init Progname** - Initializes the EXE name to the name of the source
code file name.

**Add Manifest Resource** - This Manifest Resource allows the most up to
date Common Control (OCX) objects to be used; if unselected, the Common
Controls revert to Windows 98 format. If the Manifest Resource is
included, some properties of some OCX objects become inoperative (e.g.
ForeColor for Buttons).

**Large Address Aware** - If selected, this allows the compiled program
to use up to 4GB of RAM. See [\$LargeAddressAware](LargeAddress.htm) for
more details.

**Note** -- In case of compiling a GLL or LG32 library, you can still
use the Program tab. The project can still be compiled to an EXE even if
it is an editor extension or library. In case of an extension all
**Gfa\_** statements are ignored. It is possible to create a project
that combines the functionality of a program and a GLL. For instance, a
program might contain the logic to search for text in files. The project
might then contain an interface to start the search from within a normal
program. Additionally, the program may contain a GLL interface (keyboard
shortcut or menu event) that starts the search logic as an editor
extension.

### Version info tab

In the \'Version Info\' tab don\'t forget to press the small button
with + to increment the file version number once a day.

To obtain help on the Version Info tab elements use the What\'s This
Help button \[?\] in the title of the box.

### See Also

[The Compiler Tab](idecompilerprop.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 18/09/2021 by James Gaite}

#  MouseCursor Object {#mousecursor-object}

### Purpose

Object that holds cursor.

### Syntax

**MouseCursor**

### Description

The **MouseCursor** object only has one property.

- **Handle** - Returns the hCursor handle of the cursor.(read-only).

A **MouseCursor** object is returned by the **LoadCursor** function.
Normally, the cursor is assigned to the **MouseCursor** property
directly. The custom icon is used when the **MousePointer** property is
set to 98 (**basCursor**).

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

OpenW 1 
Local mc As MouseCursor 
If Exist(WinDir & "\Cursors\hourglas.ani" ) // Only included up to WinXP 
Set mc = LoadCursor(WinDir & "\Cursors\hourglas.Ani" ) 
Else 
Set mc = LoadCursor(WinDir & "\Cursors\aero_busy.ani" ) 
EndIf 
Set Win_1.MouseCursor = mc 
Win_1 . MousePointer = 98     // basCursor 
Print Win_1.MouseCursor.Handle 
Do 
Sleep 
Until IsNothing(Me) 
Set mc = Nothing 
```

### Remarks

### See Also

[LoadCursor](loadcursor.htm), [MouseIcon](mouseicon.htm),
[MousePointer](mousepointer.htm), [DefMouse](defmouse.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 02/07/2022 by James Gaite;
Other Contributors: Jean-Marie Melanson}

#  Call()(), CallX()() Command {#callcallx-command}

### Purpose

Executes a subroutine at a specified address.

### Syntax

**Call(**addr**)(\[**parameters\]**)**

**CallX(**addr**)(\[**parameters\]**)**

*x, addr:iexp\
parameters:registers*

### Description

The **Call()()** and **CallX()()** functions call a C or an assembler
subroutine at address addr%. Any arguments are passed through the pseudo
registers \_EAX, \_ECX, etc. **CallX** allows passing segment
registers - \_DS, \_ES, \_FS, and \_GS - as well.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Debug . Show 
Dim a$ = Space$( 30 ) 
Call (LabelAddr( xMemClr )) ( _EDI = V: a$ , _ECX = 30 ) 
Trace a$ 
If 0 
xMemClr:  . mov al, 67 :  . rep stosb : . ret 
EndIf 
```

### Remarks

**Call()()** and **CallX()()** don\'t use the stack. A subroutine should
return with a simple *ret* instruction.

### See Also

[LC](lc.htm):(), [P](p.htm):(), [LP](lp.htm):(), [CCall](c.htm#ccall)(),
[LCCall](lc.htm#lccall)(), [PasCall](16vjvlg.htm)(),
[LPasCall](lp.htm#18fgnf8)(), [StdCall](stdcall.htm)(),
[LStdCall](stdcall.htm)()

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 24/09/2014 by James Gaite}

#  \_Instance Variable,  hInstance Property (App) {#instance-variable-hinstance-property-app}

### Purpose

Return the instance handle of the running program.

### Syntax

Handle = \_**Instance**

Handle = *App*.**hInstance**

### Description

For \'interpreted\' programs, the instance of the GFA-BASIC 32 IDE is
returned. For a compiled program, the instance of the program is
returned. Some Windows\'s API functions need this handle.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Dim h As Handle 
h = _INSTANCE : Print h 
h = App.hInstance  : Print h 
```

### Remarks

Within GFA-BASIC 32 there are no interpreted programs, only compiled.
However, when a program is run in the context of the IDE, the instance
handle determines the GFA-BASIC 32 IDE.

### See Also

[App](app.htm), [PrevInstance](PrevInstance.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 21/12/2021 by James Gaite}

#  FileName,  Name,  Path Properties (App) {#filename-name-path-properties-app}

### Purpose

Return or set the filename and path of the current running application.

### Syntax

\$ = App.**FileName**

\$ = App.**Name**

\$ = App.**Path**

### Description

**FileName** gets or sets (current) complete filename.

**Name** gets or sets (current) application name.

**Path** gets or sets (current) application path.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Debug . Show 
Trace App.FileName 
Trace App.Name 
Trace App.Path 
```

### Remarks

The complete path name can also be obtained from **\_CmdLine**.

**ProgName**\[\$\] returns the directory of the current running
application. In the IDE the name of GFA-BASIC 32 is returned.

### See Also

[App](app.htm), \_[CmdLine](lbtve3.htm),
[ProgName](shortprogname.htm#9huc_1)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 05/10/2014 by James Gaite}

#  Card Data Type {#card-data-type}

### Purpose

A 16 bit unsigned integral value.

### Syntax

Dim name1 As **Card**

### Description

This data type does not have declaration character.

Range: 0 - 65535

Arithmetic operations are faster with integral types than with other
data types. They are fastest with the 32-bit Integer types.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Dim d As Card = 2 
Local Card d1 
```

### Remarks

The unsigned integral types are **Byte** (8-bit) and **Card** (16-bit).

### See Also

[Boolean](boolean.htm), [Byte](byte.htm), [Card](#15nozsx),
[Short](int16.htm), [Word](int16.htm), [Int16](int16.htm),
[Long](integer.htm), [Int](integer.htm), [Integer](integer.htm),
[Int32](integer.htm), [Int64](int64.htm), [Large](int64.htm#large),
[Single](single.htm), [Double](double.htm), [Currency](currency.htm),
[Date](date.htm), [Handle](handle.htm), [String](string.htm),
[Variant](variant.htm), [Object](_7.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 24/09/2014 by James Gaite}

#  Problem with menu events {#problem-with-menu-events}

When a dialog box is created and showed as a result of processing a menu
event, the dialog box is overlapped by the GFA-BASIC 32 IDE immediately.
The IDE is brought to foreground after showing the dialog. This is a
direct result from the internal WM_COMMAND handling of the IDE: it
simply set the focus to the editor after handling the WM_COMMAND
message, e.g. the menu event. It doesn\'t check for a visible dialog
box. This problem only occurs after a menu event, not with a keyboard
event.

One workaround could be by posting the keyboard shortcut that creates
the dialog.

 

Sub menuDialog ( i% )

SendKeys \"\^+9\"        \'calls Gfa_Ex_9 eventually

EndSub

This way the keyboard shortcut is placed in the message queue and will
not be retrieved before the menu event is handled completely and the
main message loop is re-entered.

Another solution is to relocate the ShowDialog command to the Gfa_CB
procedure. Then, rather than invoke **ShowDialog** and *SetFocus* post a
WM_USER message to display dialog. The advantage of this approach is
that the initialization of the dialog box can be done in the Gfa_CB as
well. The entire handling of the dialog box can be combined in one
procedure.

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 21/10/2014 by James Gaite}

#  OnHelp Event (CommDlg) {#onhelp-event-commdlg}

### Purpose

Occurs when the Help button on a common dialog is selected.

### Syntax

Sub *CommDlg*\_**OnHelp**

### Description

If you\'ve created a Help file for your application you can use
**ShowHelp** to invoke WinHelp, or use the example in [Acessing HTMLHelp
Files](HTMLHelpFiles.htm#CallHelp) for .chm files, to display help.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

OpenW 1 
Ocx CommDlg cd 
cd.Flags = cdfScreenFonts | cdfShowHelp 
cd.ShowFont 
CloseW 1 
 
Sub cd_OnHelp 
Me . Caption = "Help Requested" 
EndSub 
```

### See Also

[CommDlg](commdlg.htm), [ShowHelp](showhelp.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 16/07/2015 by James Gaite}

# P:()(),  PasCall() Function {#p-pascall-function}

### Purpose

executes a subroutine at a specified address and returns a Long value.

### Syntax

x = **P:(**addr**)(\[**parameters\]**)**

x = **PasCall(**addr**)(\[**parameters\]**)**

*x:iexp\
addr:iexp\
parameters:aexp*

### Description

The parameters are placed in reverse order on the stack. **P:()()** and
**PasCall()()** expects the subroutine to clear the stack.

a% = P:(addr%)(1, 2, 3)

12\[esp\] 1

8\[esp\] 2

4\[esp\] 3

\[esp\] Return address

the called routine must end with ret 12, correcting the stack pointer.

The parameters can be coerced to a specific format using by preceding
the value with one of the following designators:

Dbl: double\
Sng: float, single\
Large: Large integer\
Cur: Currency value\
L: Long\
Int: Integer\
Var: Variant

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Dim a% = ProcAddr( test ) 
~ P:( a% )( Large: 2 , 3 ) 
' or 
~ PasCall( a% )( Large: 2 , 3 ) 
 
Procedure test ( i% , la As Large ) 
Print la , i% 
EndProc 
```

### Remarks

A **Procedure** takes it parameters by value using the **StdCall**
convention.

### See Also

[C](c.htm):(), [LC](lc.htm):(), [LP](lp.htm):(), [Call](call.htm)(),
[CallX](12t5n6k.htm#callx)(), [CCall](c.htm#ccall)(),
[LCCall](lc.htm#lccall)(), [LPasCall](lp.htm#18fgnf8)(),
[StdCall](stdcall.htm)(), [LStdCall](stdcall.htm)()

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 21/10/2014 by James Gaite}

#  Constants and Enumerations {#constants-and-enumerations}

Constants are a way to use meaningful names in place of a value that
does not change. Constants store values that, as the name implies,
remain constant throughout the execution of an application. You can use
constants to provide meaningful names, instead of numbers, making your
code more readable.

Enumerations provide a convenient way to work with sets of related
constants, and to associate constant values with names. For example, you
can declare an enumeration for a set of integer constants associated
with the days of the week, and then use the names of the days rather
than their integer values in your code.

### Constants

\[**Global**\] **Const** name \[**As** type\] = value

Constants declared without Global or Public are local when used inside a
procedure and global when used in the main part. Global constants can be
declared anywhere in the program and are public to the entire program.
Const and Dim are the same, except that Const prevents the variable to
be changed.

If you do not specify a type, the constant takes the data type from the
value. If you specify both type and initializer, the data type of
initializer must be convertible to type. If neither data type nor
initializer is present, the data type defaults to a 32-bits integer
(Int, Integer, Int32, and Long).

Const WM_USER = 0x400  \' a 32-bits constant

In the next example the constant takes the data type String.

Const GFA = \"Basic\"  \' a string constant

Or, more explicitly

Const GFA As String = \"Basic\"

As data type are all intrinsic GFA-BASIC 32 data types allowed.

Const Updated = \#12.07.1996#    \' becomes a Date data type

Const pi1 = 3.14                \' becomes a Double data type

Const pi2 = 3.14!               \' becomes a Single data type

Const currV = 15.20@            \' @ forces a Currency value

Const key As Short = \$10        \' declared as short

You can use an expression to be assigned to the constant. The expression
can be any combination of literals, other constants that are already
defined, and enumeration members that are already defined. You can use
arithmetic and logical operators to combine such elements. You cannot
use variables or user defined functions in initializer. However, you can
use conversion functions such as CByte and CShort, and GFA-BASIC 32
intrinsic functions.

Const WM_QUIT = WM_CLOSE + 2    \' 32-bits

Const Pi2     = PI / 2          \' Double

Const PiViertel = Atn( 1 )        \' Double, intrinsic function Atn

More than one constant can be listed

Const WM_USER = 0x400 , WM_PAINT = 15 , WM_CLOSE = \$10 , WM_QUIT =
WM_CLOSE + 2

With the types Short=Word=Int16, Card and Byte=Int8 GFA-BASIC 32
performs an overflow check at compile time. Note When no type is
specified GFA-BASIC 32 looks for the best fitting type starting with
Integer, followed by Large, and when the value is still too large, a
Double.

### Enumerations

GFA-BASIC 32 lets you create enumerations. The use of enumerations can
simplify certain programming tasks and make your program code easier to
read. You create an enumeration with the Enum keyword. The constants are
automatically assigned numerical values in order, starting with 0.

\[**Global**\] **Enum** name \[ = value\] \[,name \[ = value\]\]

Public Enum flVanilla , flChocolate , flCoffee , flStrawberry

This results in the constant flVanilla being equal to 0, flClocolate
being equal to 1, and so on. Usually the actual numerical values of the
constants in an enumeration do not matter, but if you want to assign
specific values you can:

Enum WM_NULL , WM_CREATE , WM_DESTROY , WM_MOVE ,  WM_SIZE = 5 ,
WM_ACTIVATE , WM_SETFOCUS , WM_KILLFOCUS , \_

WM_ENABLE = \$A , WM_SETREDRAW , WM_SETTEXT ,  WM_GETTEXT ,
WM_GETTEXTLENGTH , WM_PAINT , WM_CLOSE , \_

WM_QUERYENDSESSION , WM_QUIT , WM_QUERYOPEN ,  WM_ERASEBKGND ,
WM_SYSCOLORCHANGE , WM_ENDSESSION , \_

WM_SHOWWINDOW = \$18 , WM_WININICHANGE = \$1a

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 26/09/2014 by James Gaite}

#  GLL Example: Convert Characters {#gll-example-convert-characters}

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Convert characters to uppercase using Gfa_Insert.
Convert one or more characters on one or more lines to uppercase. When there is no selection the character on the right of the cursor is selected and changed to uppercase. Since Gfa_Insert is used to replace the selection, the conversion can cross line boundaries. Gfa_Right and Gfa_Insert are both actions that are stored in the Undo buffer, which can take 64 actions. As such, Gfa_Ex_V can only be undone 32 times.
 
Sub Gfa_Ex_V 
Local Int ln , c 
If Gfa_IsSelection 
Gfa_Insert Upper(Gfa_Selection) 
Else 
ln = Gfa_Line : c = Gfa_Col 
Gfa_Right 
Gfa_SelLine = ln : Gfa_SelCol = c 
Gfa_Insert Upper(Gfa_Selection) 
EndIf 
EndSub 
The difference with the previous example is that when no selection is available there is no temporary selection set as well. This reduces the number of undo actions.
 
Sub Gfa_App_U 
If Gfa_IsSelection 
Gfa_Insert Upper(Gfa_Selection) 
Else 
Gfa_Right 
If Gfa_Col 
Gfa_Replace Upper(Mid(Gfa_Text, Gfa_Col - 1 , 1 )) 
EndIf 
EndIf 
EndSub 
{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 08/10/2014 by James Gaite}
```

#  Restrictions and Features {#restrictions-and-features}

Not all GFA-BASIC 32 commands and functions may be used in an editor
extension. A GLL differs in quite some ways from a normal GFA-BASIC 32
application. GFA-BASIC 32 applications are constructed around the OLE
wrappers for windows, forms and controls. This means that maintaining
all GUI items is performed through COM calls. The forms and windows are
COM containers for the ActiveX controls provided in the GfaWin23.OCX.
The GFA-BASIC 32 application is able to communicate between the COM
items through the [Sleep](sleep.htm) command, which is used in any
normal program.

The editor extension is an external compiled GFA-BASIC 32 program in a
special format. The editor extensions are programmed in GFA-BASIC 32 and
make use of many library functions provided by GFA-BASIC 32, but a GLL
is not a COM plug-in, it is not COM based. After loading a GLL plug-in
it will become part of the IDE, its functions are called from inside the
IDE, which is a regular WINAPI program and not a COM program. The IDE
has no knowledge what so ever about COM containers and OCXs. As a
consequence a GLL cannot use [Form](form.htm), [OpenW](openw.htm),
[Ocx](ocx.htm), [Sleep](sleep.htm), etc. The general rule is: don\'t use
GFA-BASIC 32 GUI commands and don\'t use GFA-BASIC 32 specific message
loops, not even [GetEvent](getevent.htm) in a GLL.

Editor Extensions do not have a data segment, because they are nothing
more then a piece of compiled code that is recognized by the IDE. Also,
a GLL is not a DLL, it cannot contain data and cannot contain resources.
Therefore, **Read, Data, Restore, and LabelAddr()** are not allowed.
Since a GLL has no data section, inline resource files (:Files) are not
allowed as well. (There is a workaround to include data in a GLL. By
encoding binary data in a [MimeEncode](MiMeUUE.htm)\$ format, the data
can be assigned to a string variable and later decoded.)

Allowed are mostly all other GFA-BASIC 32 functions. You can open files,
use the non GUI COM objects [App](app.htm), [Screen](screen.htm),
[Debug](debug.htm), [Err](err.htm), [Printer](printer.htm), Collection,
and [DisAsm](disasm.htm). All Windows API functions may be used, there
are hardly any limitations.

You can display (test) results using **Debug.Print**,
[Trace](trace.htm), [Assert](assert.htm), and [MsgBox](msgbox.htm), or
by inserting text into program text, as well as by changing the status
bar text [Gfa_StatusText](gfa_statustext.htm)**=**.

Simple input for a GLL can take place with [Gfa_KeyGet](gfa_keyget.htm),
[InputBox](inputbox.htm)**,** [Prompt](prompt.htm)**, or**
[Popup](popup.htm).

This manual contains many examples that clarify the usage of GFA-BASIC
32 statements in GLLs.

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/05/14 by James Gaite}

#  Other Window Commands in GLLs {#other-window-commands-in-glls}

Everything that has to do with [OpenW](openw.htm),
[LoadForm](loadform.htm), [OCX](ocx.htm), [Sleep](sleep.htm),
[GetEvent](getevent.htm), [MENU](menuarr.htm)(), etc is not allowed.
However, for easy access, the following GFA-BASIC statements and
functions are implemented to be used in Gfa Editor Extensions.

**ExtensionImplementation**

[Dlg](dlg.htm)(id) - Obtains the window handle of the dialog with number
id (0 .. 31)

[DlgItem](dlgitem.htm)(id,idc) - Obtains the window handle of the child
control idc in dialogbox id.

c\$ = [\_Win\$](winstr.htm)(h) - Returns a string with the window text
of the window with handle h.

[\_Win\$](winstr.htm)(h) = c\$ - Sets the window text of window h with
the contents of string c\$.

[MoveW](movew.htm) id, x, y -
*SetWindowPos*(Dlg(id),0,x,y,0,0,SWP_NOZORDER \| SWP_NOSIZE)

[SizeW](sizew.htm) id, w, h -
*SetWindowPos*(Dlg(id),0,0,0,w,h,SWP_NOZORDER \| SWP_NOMOVE)

[CloseW](closew.htm) id - *DestroyWindow*(Dlg(id))

[ClearW](clearw.htm) id - *InvalidateRect*(Dlg(id),0,1) +
UpdateWindow(Dlg(id))

[ShowW](showw.htm) id,swf - *ShowWindow*(Dlg(id), swf)

[EnableW](enablew.htm) id - *EnableWindow*(Dlg(id), 1)

[DisableW](disablew.htm) id - *EnableWindow*(Dlg(id), 0)

[Enabled?](enabledq.htm)(id) - *IsWindowEnabled*(Dlg(id))

[SetCheck](setcheck.htm) id, n, f - *SendMessage*(Dlg(id,n),
BM_SETCHECK,f,0)

[Check?](check.htm)(id, n) - f = *SendMessage(* Dlg(id,n),
BM_GETCHECK,0,0)

[Zoomed?](zoomed.htm)(id) - *IsZoomed*(Dlg(id))

[Visible?](4olpp5.htm)(id) *- IsWindowVisible*(Dlg(id))

[Iconic?](iconic.htm)(id) *- IsIconic*(Dlg(id))

Except for [Dialog](dialog.htm)**\#**, [ShowDialog](showdialog.htm), and
[CloseDialog](closedialog.htm), all functions take either a dialog
number (between 0 and 31) or a window handle.

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/05/14 by James Gaite}

#  Compiling and Installing {#compiling-and-installing}

When a project is marked as a GLL project (.GLL is the extension of a
compiled GFA Editor Extension file) the Compile-Dialog box displays an
additional tab, called \'Create editor gll\'. The tab provides an easy
way to initialize the name of the GLL.

[The Create Exe Dialog Box](42.ycd5.htm)

[Installing the GLL](ggl74y.htm)

[Assigning the Keys](31c3yut.htm)

[Testing a GLL](3lg8iz.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/05/14 by James Gaite}

#  The Form Editor {#the-form-editor}

To create a window or dialog box, you must create a form to contain
controls, add controls to the form, set properties for the controls, and
write code that responds to form and control events.

·To activate the Form Editor either use F7 or click the button on the
toolbar.

A new, empty Form is displayed. Use the Properties window to set
properties for the Form - that is, to change the name, behavior, and
appearance of the form. For example, to change the caption on a form,
set the Caption property.

### Add a control

Use the Toolbox to add controls to the form. The Toolbox is always
visible in the Form Editor. To see the name of a particular control in
the Toolbox, position the mouse pointer over that control.

To add a control, find the control you want to add in the Toolbox, drag
the control onto the form, and then drag one or more of the control\'s
adjustment handles until the control is the size and shape you want. The
element is placed with a default size. However, to size a control while
you add it, place the pointer where you want the upper-left corner of
the control to be, then drag to the right and down until the control is
the size you want.

To copy a control hold down the Ctrl key and click the control to be
copied.

To delete a control or form simply select the control that you wish to
delete and then press the Delete-key. You can also right-click on the
control or form and select Kill Ocx/Form from the context menu. A
Message box will appear to make sure whether you really want to delete
the item. Answering \'Yes\' will delete the item permanently.

After you\'ve added controls to the form, use the commands in the
context menu (right click) to adjust the alignment and spacing of the
controls. Hold down the Shift-key to select multiple controls.

### The context menu

The Form editor contains special tools for layout. These tools help
align and arrange controls in the correct place. The Form editor tools
are collected in a context menu, which differs from situation to
situation. The context menu shows the tools applicable for the selected
form, control, or controls.

The first line of the context menu displays the form or control(s) that
are affected. This line is dimmed so that it cannot be selected.

One of the options from the context menu is \"Align to Grid\". When you
are placing or arranging controls in a form, you can use the layout grid
(8 pixels) for more precise positioning. When this option is turned on,
controls appear to "snap to" the dotted lines of the grid as if
magnetized. You can turn this "snap to grid" feature on and off and
change the size of the layout grid cells.

![](../images/FormEditor.png){border="0"}

### Tab order

Use the \'Ocx Overview\' dialog box (View menu) to set the tab order of
the controls on the form. GFA-BASIC 32 determines the tab order by the
order the controls are placed on the form (there is no *TabIndex*
property). The tab order can be changed by dragging the controls in the
\'Ocx Overview\' dialog box. If you want to prevent users from tabbing
to a particular control, you can set the **TabStop** property to
**False** for that control, but only in code.

### Order of creation

The tab order determines order of creation. The last control created has
the topmost attribute, it is placed highest in the Z-order and is drawn
over other controls. The visibility or the Z-order can be set using the
context menu of the selected control. A partly visible control can be
put on top by selecting \"Put on Top\", and a control at the top of the
Z-order can be placed behind another control by selecting \"Send Back\".
The tab order is immediately updated in the \"Ocx Overview\" window. The
control number (#) in the context menu shows the creation order.

### Ocx on Ocx

Some OCXs can be used as parent control. Assigning controls to a parent
makes sure that they are in the correct Z-order and that they can be
moved together with their parent. To assign a control to a parent,
select the parent BEFORE adding a control to the form. Right-click
somewhere in the form and select from the context menu the parent of the
next control. In the figure above, the new control will be added to Form
frm1 directly. To add the control to the Frame fr1 select \"Ocx on
(Frame) fr1\". The Ocx Overview window reflects the owner-child
relationship by adding a branch to the parent OCX. See also OcxOcx

### Known Issues

1.  *\[Fixed in version 2.58\]* Moving the form across the screen can
    leave a trail of pixels streaming from the top left corner -- this
    can be cleared by minimizing and then restoring the
    window.\[Reported by James Gaite, 17/05/2020\]
2.  *\[Fixed in version 2.58\]* When you first open a file with
    pre-drawn forms and enter the Form Editor, the Ocx objects appear as
    blank spaces on the grey grid; once again, this can be cleared by
    minimizing then restoring the IDE window. \[Reported by James Gaite,
    17/05/2020\]
3.  *\[Fixed in version 2.58\]* Screen corruption can occur in maximised
    windows: to simulate, open the IDE, maximise, add a command object
    to form, restore the window, then maximise again and the screen is a
    mess. Once again, this can be cleared by minimizing and restoring
    the IDE window.\[Reported by James Gaite, 17/05/2020\]

Next:[The Toolbox](1p3yzfn.htm)\
[Creating a Control](8g.ket.htm)\
[Setting OCX Properties](qe6miu.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 16/08/2020 by James Gaite}

#  Editor Extension Reference {#editor-extension-reference}

GFA-BASIC 32 enables you to automate development tasks in the GFA-BASIC
32 development environment. To access the IDE about 130 commands and
functions are available. This section describes each command and
function

[Keypress Event Subs](gfa_ex.htm)

[Cursor Movement](gfa_left.htm)

[Find & Replace](gfa_replacetext.htm)

[BookMarks](gfa_nextbookmark.htm)

[Ctrl + Key Shortcuts](gfa_ctrly.htm)

[New, Loading and Printing](gfa_mergefile.htm)

[Save Project File](gfa_saveprefile.htm)

[Procedures](gfa_procline.htm)

[Syntax Checking](gfa_changed.htm)

Menu bar

IDE Information

Register Functions

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/05/14 by James Gaite}

#  GLL Example: AutoSave {#gll-example-autosave}

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

The original GFA implementation of the auto save feature.
 
Sub Gfa_Init 
' 
' Add menu item for AutoSave 
' 
Global Int IdxAutosave = Gfa_AddMenu( "&AutoSave" , Menu_Autosave ) 
Gfa_MenuCheck ( idxautosave ) = Gfa_IntSetting( "Auto_Save" ) And 1 
Gfa_MenuDesc ( idxautosave ) = "Autosave every 5 minutes" 
EndSub 
 
Sub Menu_Autosave ( idx% ) 
Gfa_MenuCheck ( idx ) = !Gfa_MenuCheck( idx ) 
Gfa_IntSetting ( "Auto_Save" ) = -Gfa_MenuCheck( Idx ) 
EndSub 
 
Sub Gfa_Minute          // autosave every 5 Minutes 
Local Date d 
Local st_old$ 
// Don't save if not wanted 
If !Gfa_MenuCheck( IdxAutosave ) Then Exit Sub 
// Don't save empty program 
If Gfa_LineCnt == 0 Then Exit Sub 
// Changes have bee saved before 
If !Gfa_Dirty Then Exit Sub 
// Get filetime of the autosave file 
Try 
d = FileDateTime(TempDir & "temp.g32" ) 
Catch 
d = 0.0 
EndCatch 
// When not older then 4.5 Minuten 
// (1 day / 24 (hours) / 60 (Minutes)) * 4.5 
// so 5 minutes or older is 
If d > Now - ( 1 / 24 / 60 ) * 4.5 
Exit Sub    //do nothing 
EndIf 
// Change statusbar 
st_old = Gfa_StatusText 
Gfa_StatusText = "Autosave as " & TempDir & "temp.g32" 
Gfa_SaveFile TempDir & "temp.g32" 
Gfa_StatusText = st_old 
End Sub 
{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 08/10/2014 by James Gaite}
```

#  The Toolbox {#the-toolbox}

Use the Toolbox to add controls to the form. The Toolbox is always
visible in the Form Editor. To see the name of a particular control in
the Toolbox, position the mouse pointer over that control.

![](../images/bm9.bmp){border="0"}

The first icon in the Toolbox displays a mouse pointer. The pointer is
meant for resizing controls and changing their attributes. To change the
size of an element, simply click on one of the corners of an element and
resize the surrounding box.

The toolbox contains the following OCX controls:

| Ocx Control | Description |
|:---|:---|
| [Command](_3.htm) | A pushbutton is the best known button available in Windows. It is just a button where the user can click on. As long as the mouse-key is not released, the button\'s visible state is changed (it looks \'pressed\' down). |
| [Option](option.htm) | Also known as Radio button. Option buttons are normally \'grouped\' together in a Groupbox (Frame). Only one radio button can be selected within one group. |
| [Checkbox](checkbox.htm) | normally can be switched either off (empty) or on (with an X-mark in it). Use this control to give the user a True/False or Yes/No option. |
| [Label](label.htm) | displays text that a user can\'t change directly. |
| [Image](image.htm) | control can display a graphic from a bitmap, icon, or metafile, as well as enhanced metafile, JPEG, or GIF files. |
| [Textbox](textbox.htm) | displays information entered at design time, entered by the user, or assigned to the control in code |
| [RichEdit](richedit.htm) | enter and edit text while also providing more advanced formatting features than the conventional TextBox control. |
| [ImageList](imagelist.htm) | a repository of images for use by other OCXs and by controls with a Picture property. |
| [TreeView](treeview.htm) | displays data in a hierarchical in nature |
| [ListView](listview.htm) | displays data as ListItem objects. Each ListItem object can have an optional icon associated with the label of the object. |
| [Timer](timer.htm) | execute code at regular intervals by causing a Timer event to occur. |
| [ProgressBar](progressbar.htm) | graphically represents the progress of a transaction. |
| [Scroll](scroll.htm) | easy navigation through a long list of items or a large amount of information. |
| [Slider](slider.htm) | consists of a scale, defined by the Min and Max properties, and a \"thumb,\" which the end user can manipulate using the mouse or arrow keys. |
| [ToolBar](toolbar.htm) | a frame containing a collection of Button objects. |
| [StatusBar](statusbar.htm) | a frame that can consist of several panels which inform the user of the status of an application. |
| [ListBox](listbox.htm) | displays a list of items from which the user can select one or more. |
| [ComboBox](combobox.htm) | combines the features of a TextBox control and a ListBox control-users can enter information in the text box portion or select an item from the list box portion of the control. |
| [Frame](frame.htm) | provides an identifiable grouping for controls. You can also use a Frame to subdivide a form functionally-for example, to separate groups of Option controls. |
| [CommDlg](commdlg.htm) | provides a standard set of dialog boxes for operations such as opening and saving files, setting print options, and selecting colors and fonts. The control also has the ability to display help by running the Windows Help engine |
| [Form](form.htm)(control) | a control with the same features as a Form (use as PictureBox for instance). |
| [MonthView](monthview.htm) | to view and set date information via a calendar-like interface. |
| [TabStrip](tabstrip.htm) | acts like the dividers in a notebook or the labels on a group of file folders. By using a TabStrip control, you can define multiple pages for the same area of a window or dialog box in your application. |
| [TrayIcon](trayicon.htm) | creates a taskbar notification icon. |
| [Animation](animation.htm) | displays silent Audio Video Interleaved (AVI) clips. |
| [UpDown](updown.htm) | a pair of arrow buttons that the user can click to increment or decrement a value, such as a scroll position or a number displayed in a *buddy* control. |
| [Form](form.htm) | New Form (or press Shift + F7 or select the menu item \'New Form\') |

Several OCX controls can be used as parent OCX:

**Image** - A container with a small resource footprint. This could be
used instead of a Form, which uses more resources (scaling, a DC, a
Picture).

**Form** - A Form OCX can be used as a container (of course).

**Frame** - Particular useful for Option OCXs (Radio Buttons). The
**.Transparent** property of the Frame may not be changed; otherwise,
the embedded controls are invalid.

**TabStrip** - To embed (for instance) a Frame Ocx.

**ToolBar** - To embed (for instance) a ComboBox Ocx.

**StatusBar** - To embed (for instance) a Command Ocx

Next:[Creating a Control](8g.ket.htm)

[Setting OCX Properties](qe6miu.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 12/05/14 by James Gaite}

#  Clear,  GetFormat,  GetData,  SetData,  GetPict,  SetPict,  GetText,  SetText Methods {#clear-getformat-getdata-setdata-getpict-setpict-gettext-settext-methods}

### Purpose

Controls the **ClipBoard** object

### Syntax

*ClipBoard.***Clear**

*boolean* = *ClipBoard.***GetFormat**(format)

*object* = *ClipBoard.***GetData**(\[format\])

*ClipBoard.***SetData** Picture \[,format\]

*picture* = *ClipBoard.***GetPict**(\[format\])

*ClipBoard.***SetPict** Picture \[,format\]

*string* = *ClipBoard.***GetText**(\[format\])

*ClipBoard.***SetText** text\$ \[,format\]

### Description

**Clear** Clears the clipboard.

**GetFormat**(format) returns a Boolean indicting the presence of data
of the specified format on the clipboard.

**GetData**(\[format\]) gets a graphic from the clipboard. Return type
is a **Picture**.

**SetData** Picture \[,format\] puts a **Picture** on the clipboard.

**GetPict**(\[format\]) gets a graphic from the clipboard. Return type
is a **Picture**.

**SetPict** Picture \[,format\] puts a **Picture** on the clipboard.

**GetText**(\[format\]) gets text from the clipboard. Return type is a
String

**SetText** text\$ \[,format\] puts text on the clipboard.

If *format* is 0 or omitted, GFA-BASIC 32 automatically uses the
appropriate format when saving data to the clipboard when using
**Set....** commands or 0 (zero) when using **Get....** functions.
Otherwise *format* can be any appropriate CF\_\* constant representing a
clipboard format, some examples:

|                  |                                 |
|:-----------------|:--------------------------------|
| CF_TEXT (1)      | ( Default) Text                 |
| CF_BITMAP (2)    | Bitmap (.bmp files)             |
| CF_METAFILE (3)  | Metafile (.wmf files)           |
| CF_RTF (\$BF01)  | Rich Text Format (.rtf file)    |
| CF_DIB (8)       | Device-independent bitmap (DIB) |
| CF_HTML (\$C0A9) | UTF-8 HTML Format               |

**NOTE:** Care should always be taken when using the *format* parameter
as an incorrect value or an unexpected data type on the clipboard can
cause an error.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

Local a$ = "GFABasic32" , hwnd As Handle , n As Int32 , pic As Picture , pic2 As Picture 
Local r$ = "{\rtf1\ansi\ansicpg1252\deff0\deflang2057{\fonttbl{\f0\fnil\fcharset0 MS Shell Dlg;}}\viewkind4\uc1\pard\f0\fs17 GFABasic32\par}" 
Debug . Show 
// Clear Clipboard and copy r$ in RTF format... 
Clipboard . Clear 
Clipboard . SetText r$ , $BF01     ' $BF01 = RTF 
// ...then confirm that data on clipboard is RTF and retrieve 
Trace Clipboard.GetFormat( $BF01 ) 
Trace Clipboard.GetText( $BF01 ) 
// Copy a$ to clipboard in plain text... 
Clipboard . SetText a$            /* can also be Clipboard.SetText a$ , 1 
Trace Clipboard.SetText( a$ , 1 )  /* or ~Clipboard.SetText(a$) 
// ...and retrieve the plain text from the clipboard 
Trace Clipboard.GetText( 1 )      ' 1 = Text 
' 
// Print a$ 15 times down left side of window and capture as a bitmap... 
For n = 1 To 15 : Print a$ : Next n 
Get 0 , 0 , 60 , 200 , hwnd 
Set pic = CreatePicture( hwnd , False) 
// ...copy to clipboard... 
Clipboard . SetPict pic 
// ...then retrieve in a different picture object... 
Set pic2 = Clipboard.GetPict( 2 ) ' 2 = Bitmap 
// ...and display in window to right of original 
PaintPicture pic2 , 100 , 0 
// Same as above but using GetData 
Set pic2 = Clipboard.GetData( 2 ) ' Same as GetPict(2) 
PaintPicture pic2 , 200 , 0 
```

### Remarks

The **Set....** commands can also be written as functions in the
following format:

\~Clipboard.SetText( a\$ , 1 )

However, as no value is returned, they seem to be of no practical use.

### See Also

[ClipBoard](clipboard.htm)

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 21/07/2019 by James Gaite}

#  \* AddressOf Operator {#addressof-operator}

### Purpose

Used to obtain the memory/descriptor address of a variable.

### Syntax

% = **\***var

*var: variable*

### Description

The **\*** operator is used as the address-of operator like **V:** (and
**VarPtr**) and **ArrPtr**.

For variable-length strings and arrays **\*** returns the address of the
descriptor and behaves as **ArrPtr**(). For all other variables the
**\*** operator returns the memory address of data contained in that
variable.

For a fixed string **\*** returns the first four bytes of the data. This
function has <u>no meaning</u> for a fixed-string.

### Example

``` gfb
$Library "UpdateRT"
UpdateRuntime ' Patches GfaWin23.Ocx

OpenW 1 
Global x% , y$ 
Print * x% 
KeyGet y$  // Press a key to end 
CloseW 1 
```

### Remarks

The **\*** operator is synonym to **ArrPtr** for strings and arrays, and
**VarPtr**() and **V:** for other variables, fixed strings excluded.

### See Also

[ArrPtr](arrptr.htm), [Varptr](varptr.htm), [V](varptr.htm):

{Created by Sjouke Hamstra; Last updated: 17/09/2014 by James Gaite}

#  The Menu Bar {#the-menu-bar}

The menu bar of the IDE is divided in several sub menus. Depending on
the version of the IDE you are using, the organization of the menu items
might differ - the description below is for version 2.54 onwards.

### The File menu [Show](javascript:pr(%22FileMenuhl%22,%22FileMenu%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#FileMenuhl}  {#the-file-menu-show}

::: {#FileMenu style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
The File menu (Alt + F) is used to load, save, and print GFA-BASIC
programs.

- **New** - deletes the program from memory. A confirmation is required
  first if there are unsaved edits.
- **Open\...** - Displays a file selector, listing the files in the
  current directory. Any files with the extension .g32 (= GFA-BASIC32
  project file) can be loaded into the editor by moving the mouse
  pointer to the relevant entry and pressing the left mouse button
  quickly twice in succession. Any programs which are happen to be
  already in memory.
- **Save** - Saves the program currently in memory in plain text format
  (extension .g32) in the current directory. If the file has been given
  a name the existing file is simply overwritten.\
  The data in the Files sidebar tab is saved in a packed and
  mime-encoded format. The same is true for the form data.
- **Save as** - Works like Save, the difference being that Save as\...
  invokes a file selector which lists all files in the current
  directory. You now have the option of saving the program under a
  different name and/or in a different directory.
- **Save to History\...** - This allows the saving of a file history to
  a ZIP file; see [Save to History](SavetoHistory.htm).
- **Insert File\...** - Allows the insertion or merger of another .g32
  or .lst file into the current program listing. A File Selector is
  shown and the desird file can be selected from it.
- **Print \...** - This allows the printing of the current program
  listing. When selected, a Printer Selector is shown allowing you to
  select the printer and change the settings before printing. Other
  options can set using the [Printer Properties](bbxmac.htm) dialog box.
- **Export** - This opens a submenu allowing you to save or copy to
  clipboard the current program in either RTF or pre-formatted HTML, or
  to send the listing by email (this last option only works if you have
  a dedicated email program installed - see
  [Gfa_SendMail](Gfa_SendMail.htm) for more information).
- *\[MRU List\]* - This is a list of up to nine of the Most Recently
  Used files loaded into the IDE. Hovering over each entry will show its
  file path in the Status Bar, whilst pressing the number key equivalent
  to the number of the file in the list will automatically load it into
  the IDE.
- **Exit** - Returns to the calling program (normally this means to
  Windows). A confirmation is required first.
:::

### Edit Menu [Show](javascript:pr(%22EditMenuhl%22,%22EditMenu%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#EditMenuhl}  {#edit-menu-show}

::: {#EditMenu style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
The Edit menu is used to find and/or replace strings within the program.
It is invoked with the shortcut ALT + E

- **Undo** - Activating Undo with the Alt + Backspace or Ctrl + Z
  shortcuts revokes all previous changes made to a program line
  providing that the cursor remained on the same line after the changes
  were made.

- **Cut** - Cut selection to clipboard (Ctrl + X).

- **Copy** - Copy selection to clipboard (Ctrl + C).

- **Paste** - Paste clipboard contents (Ctrl + V)

- **Select Word** - Selects the current word (Ctrl + W)

- **Comment Lines** - Comments the selected lines using the degree
  character. (Ctrl + I)

- **Uncomment Lines** - Uncomments the lines commented using the degree
  character. (Ctrl + J)

- **Goto Line** -
  [Show](javascript:pr(%22EditGotohl%22,%22EditGoto%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#EditGotohl}

  ::: {#EditGoto style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
  - **Goto Line..**. - Displays a Goto dialog box. You can either fill
    in an absolute line number or a relative value using -/+. (Ctrl + G)
  - **Navigate backwards** - Jumps back to the last line edited (Ctrl +
    -)
  - **Navigate forwards** - Jumps forward to the next line edited
    (follwoing the use of Navigate Backwards) (Ctrl + Shift + -)
  - **Next ErrorLine** - Jumps to the next line containing a syntax
    error (F4).
  - **Prev Errorline** - Jumps to the previous line containing a syntax
    error (Shift + F4).
  :::

- **Find & Replace**
  [Show](javascript:pr(%22EditFindhl%22,%22EditFind%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#EditFindhl}

  ::: {#EditFind style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
  - **Find** - Creates a Dialog box . You can enter the string to look
    for in the edit field after the Search For entry. Invoking the Find
    button performs a search for the given string from the current
    cursor position until the end of the program (Ctrl + F).
  - **Find Next** - Performs a search for the next occurrence of the
    string specified with Find\... starting from the current cursor
    position (F3).
  - **Find Previous** - Performs a search for the previous occurence of
    the string specified with Find, starting from the current cursor
    position (Shift + F3)
  - **Replace** - Replace text (Ctrl + R).
  - **Replace Next** - Performs a search for the next occurrence of the
    string specified with Find\... starting from the current cursor
    position and replaces it (Ctrl + F3).
  - **Replace Previous** - Performs a search/replace for the previous
    occurrence of the string specified with Find\... starting from the
    current cursor position and replaces it (Ctrl + Shift + F3).
  - **Incremental Search** - Performs a search which matches the text as
    you are typing (Ctrl + Shift + I).
  :::

- **Proc**
  [Show](javascript:pr(%22EditProchl%22,%22EditProc%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#EditProchl}

  ::: {#EditProc style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
  - **Set to Top** - Moves the cursor to the first line of the procedure
    (Ctrl + F11)
  - **Fold Others** - Folds all except the active procedure (Ctrl + F12)
  - **Fold All** - Collapse all procedures (Shift + F12)
  - **Go To Previous** - Navigates to the first line of the previous
    procedure in the program. (Ctrl + PageUp)
  - **Go To Next** - Moves the cursor to the top of the next procedure
    in th program listing.(Ctrl + PageDown)
  - **Select Proc** - Selects and highlights the procedure containing
    the cursor. (Shift + F11)
  - **Print Proc \...** - Sends the code of the active procedure to the
    printer. (Alt + F11)
  - **Insert New** - Opens a pop up box allowing the creation of a new
    Procedure, Function, \[Event\] Sub or FunctionVar. (Shift + Ctrl +
    N)  
  - **Disassembly** - Disassembles the compiled procedure and sends it
    to the Output Window. (Alt + F12)
  :::

- **Bookmarks**
  [Show](javascript:pr(%22EditBookmarkhl%22,%22EditBookmark%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#EditBookmarkhl}

  ::: {#EditBookmark style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
  - **Toggle Bookmark** - Adds bookmark to the active line if there is
    none, or removes one if there is. (Ctrl + B)
  - **Next Bookmark** - Navigates to the next Bookmark. (Ctrl + Down)
  - **Previous Bookmark** - Moves the cursor to the previous Bookmark.
    (Ctrl + Up)
  - **Clear Bookmarks** - Clears all bookmarks.
  - **Set Mark** - Allows the setting of Marks numbered 0 to 7. (Ctrl +
    K)  
  - **Goto Mark** - Moves the cursor to the specified Mark. (Ctrl + Q)\
    *See [here](bookmarks.htm) for more information on Bookmarks and
    Marks.*
  :::

- **Format Lines** - Format lines are dotted lines which are drawn
  delineating the extent of If\...Endif, For\...Next,
  Select\...EndSelect and other similar constructs which hold nested
  instructions. The elements of such a construct can be inspected by
  hovering the mouse over the format line (the cursor changes to an
  up/down arrow) and a specific format line can be highlighted by
  clicking on it, at which point the line turns from dotted black to a
  solid green and the extent of the construct is indicated by red dots
  in the margin. (F4)

- **Insert Special** - Allows the insertion of the following special
  information strings: Date, Time, Date+Time, ProcName, Gfa_FileName,
  Gfa_FileTime, Gfa_ExeName, Gfa_ExeTime, FileVersion and
  ProductVersion. (App + I)

- **Record Keys** - Starts recording keyboard keys and changes to \'Stop
  Recording\' to stop recording (Alt + Ctrl + R). See also Statusbar.

- **Play Recorded keys** - Plays back recorded keys.

- **Complete Word** - Forces the Auto-Complete box to appear. Use this
  if Auto-Complete is off but you wish to use it temporarily; it is also
  useful on the few occasions that the Auto-Complete box does not appear
  such as if you start editing in the middle of a word. (Ctrl + Space).
:::

### Project menu [Show](javascript:pr(%22ProjectMenuhl%22,%22ProjectMenu%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#ProjectMenuhl}  {#project-menu-show}

::: {#ProjectMenu style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
- **Run** - Runs the program (F5). First compiles the program (in
  memory) to collect all variables and check program integrity.
- **Syntax Check** - Compiles the program (in memory) to collect all
  variables and check program integrity (Shift + F5)
- **Cleanup** - Cleans up open windows, frees handles, and closes
  files.\
  Let\'s assume your program ends without first closing all of the
  windows opened by it. If this was a non critical program termination
  (i.e. not a crash) - for example a break invoked by pressing Control +
  Break - the GFA-BASIC editor window is shown again as the active
  window. However, all windows which were not closed by your program are
  still on the Desktop either inactive or left behind as icons. Cleanup
  closes all these \'dead\' windows and reports them to Windows in an
  orderly fashion.
- **Compile/Build** - Generates a stand-alone EXE file, a GLL extension
  file, or LG32 library. See [Create Exe](129pve8.htm).
- **Launch EXE\...** - Checks for out-of-date files, saves and compiles
  the file and, if there are no errors, the compiled file is launched.
- **New Form** - Creates a new Form in the Form Editor (Shift + F7)
- **New OCX Object** - Creates a new OCX object in the Form Editor
  (Ctrl + F7)
- **Add COM Reference** - \[Not Implemented Yet\].
:::

### Extra menu [Show](javascript:pr(%22ExtraMenuhl%22,%22ExtraMenu%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#ExtraMenuhl}  {#extra-menu-show}

::: {#ExtraMenu style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
- **Properties** - Set [Editor](ideproperties.htm),
  [Printer](bbxmac.htm), [Compiler](idecompilerprop.htm) and
  [Other](ideextraprop.htm) options. [More\...](ideproperties.htm)
- **Extension Manager** - Displays the extension manager dialog box that
  allows loading and removing GFA-BASIC 32 editor extensions.\
  See also: [GFA-BASIC 32 Editor
  Extensions](idh_gfa_basic_32_edito8c.htm).
- **Insert Packed Data \...** - Inserts a string declaration with
  mime-encoded, packed, stringdata. This allows you to store binary
  information inside the code. It works essentially the same as saving
  binary resources in the Files: sidebar section.\
  This insertion comes in handy when you want to store static data into
  a GLL or LG32 file. These file formats do not allow data storage in
  either :Files or DATA commands.
- **Memory Dump** - Displays a Prompt to provide a memory address from
  which to display a HexDump in the Output Window - the address can be
  followed by a comma and a value for the number of bytes to show
  (default = 256 bytes). Also of interest is HexDump in the gfawinx
  library which returns the memory block as a string. (Ctrl + Shift + H)
:::

### View menu [Show](javascript:pr(%22ViewMenuhl%22,%22ViewMenu%22,%22Hide%22,%22Show%22,%22block%22)){#ViewMenuhl}  {#view-menu-show}

::: {#ViewMenu style="display:none;background-color:rgb(238,238,238);"}
- **Output window** - Toggles the debug output window (Alt + 3)
- **Code editor** - Switches to the code editor when the form editor is
  active (Alt + 0)
- **Form editor** - Switches to the form editor when the code editor is
  active (Alt + 1)
- **Properties window** - Forces the creation of the OCX properties tab
  in the sidebar. (Alt + 2).
- **Split windows** - Toggles between a visible and hidden sidebar
  (Alt + 4).
